narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an impressive LSAT score of 43, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, including top-decile performance in your first year of school. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 48.8%—high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower decile ranking in Year 3 grades, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam struggle, even though your Year 1 deciles were strong. Your outstanding LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, countering that concern and pulling the prediction toward success more than any other element. Your undergraduate GPA and full-time student status also nudged things positively, though to a lesser extent, while your middle-family-income background had minimal impact. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is well above the average of around 37, which is a real strength many applicants lack, but your Year 3 deciles in the bottom range (versus averages around the middle) raised red flags that outweighed it. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted prep to boost your later-year performance, as that was the key hurdle here. You might also highlight your LSAT excellence in appeals or applications to other schools that weigh it more heavily. With your profile, targeted improvements could easily shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you've got the raw talent to succeed. We're rooting for you.
